sections.content:
The code executed 4 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 108, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 36 of about 123 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
From rows [30,31] cols [39,43]:
- UP: worked to [25,26]
- RIGHT: blocked (stayed at [25,26])
- DOWN: worked to [30,31]
- DOWN: blocked (stayed at [30,31])
So from [39,43], only UP and DOWN work (vertically). RIGHT is blocked at both [25,26] and [30,31].
This means the right corridor is a dead end for horizontal movement. The blocks can only move up and down in this corridor.
Let me reconsider the maze layout. The white cross is at rows [46,47] cols [51,52]. The blocks are currently at rows [30,31] cols [39,43]. The row difference is 15 and col difference is 8.
Looking at the gray structure boundary again:
- [39, 54], [39, 58], [54, 58], [54, 39], [50, 39], [50, 43], [49, 44], [40, 44], [40, 48], [39, 49]
The right room is bounded by:
- Top: row 39, cols 54-58
- Right: col 58, rows 39-54
- Bottom: row 54, cols 39-58
- Left: complex path from [54,39] up to [39,49]
The opening to the right room is around rows [40,49] cols [44,48]. But the blocks are 5x5, so they need a 5x5 opening. The opening seems to be at rows [40,44] cols [44,48] (based on boundary points [40,44] to [40,48]).
Wait, let me re-examine the boundary:
- [40, 44] to [40, 48]: horizontal segment at row 40, cols 44-48
- [40, 48] to [39, 49]: diagonal to row 39, col 49
- [39, 49] to [20, 49]: vertical segment at col 49, rows 20-39
So the opening is at row 40, cols 44-48. The blocks need to be at rows [40,44] (or [39,43]) to enter this opening. But the blocks are currently at rows [30,31], which is above the opening.
From [39,43], DOWN leads to [40,41] cols [29,33] (center). This is because the corridor at cols [39,43] connects to the center at rows [40,41] cols [29,33].
Wait, I think I misunderstood the movement. Let me re-examine:
- From [39,43], DOWN led to [40,41] cols [29,33]. This is a jump from cols [39,43] to cols [29,33], which is 10 columns left. This doesn't make sense for a simple DOWN movement.
Actually, I think what happened is that the blocks moved DOWN through a vertical corridor, and the corridor curved left, leading to the center. The blocks are 5x5, so they moved as a unit through the corridor, following the path.
Let me think about this differently. The maze has corridors that the blocks follow. When I move DOWN from [39,43], the blocks follow the corridor down and then left to the center. When I move UP from [29,33], the blocks follow the corridor up to [34,38], then further up to [35,36], [30,31], [25,26]. When I move RIGHT from [30,31], the blocks follow the corridor right to [39,43].
So the maze is a network of corridors, and the blocks follow the corridor path when I give a direction. The blocks can't move freely; they follow the corridor.
Given this, the question is: how do I reach the white cross at rows [46,47] cols [51,52]? The white cross is in the right room, which has an opening at rows [40,44] cols [44,48].
From the current position [39,43], I need to find a way to enter the right room. The opening is at cols [44,48], which is to the right of cols [39,43]. But RIGHT is blocked from [39,43].
Wait, maybe I need to approach the opening from a different direction. The opening is at rows [40,44] cols [44,48]. If I can get the blocks to rows [40,44], maybe RIGHT would work.
From [39,43], DOWN leads to [40,41] cols [29,33]. From [29,33], RIGHT leads to [34,38]. From [34,38], UP leads to [35,36], [30,31], [25,26]. From [30,31], RIGHT leads to [39,43].
So the loop is: [29,33] → RIGHT → [34,38] → UP → [35,36] → [30,31] → [25,26] → RIGHT → [39,43] → DOWN → [29,33]
But I need to reach the right room. Let me try a different approach: from [34,38], try DOWN instead of UP. Earlier, DOWN from [34,38] was blocked. But maybe I need to try from a different position.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the boundary. The gray structure has an opening at rows [40,44] cols [44,48]. The blocks at rows [40,41] cols [34,38] are to the left of this opening. If I move RIGHT from [34,38], the blocks would move to [39,43] (following the corridor). But the opening is at cols [44,48], which is to the right of [39,43].
Hmm, maybe the corridor at [39,43] doesn't connect to the right room. Let me look at the boundary more carefully:
- [39, 49] to [20, 49]: vertical segment at col 49
- [20, 49] to [20, 54]: horizontal segment at row 20, cols 49-54
- [20, 54] to [5, 54]: vertical segment at col 54, rows 5-20
- [5, 54] to [5, 19]: horizontal segment at row 5, cols 19-54
Wait, I need to see the full boundary. Let me get more boundary points.
Actually, I realize the boundary I printed earlier was truncated. Let me get the full boundary to understand the maze layout better.
But more importantly, I need to find a way to reach the white cross. Let me try a different approach: from [39,43], try DOWN multiple times to see if the blocks eventually reach the right room.
Wait, earlier I tried DOWN from [39,43] and it led to [40,41] cols [29,33]. But maybe if I try DOWN again from [29,33], the blocks would go to a different location.
Let me try: from [39,43], DOWN to [29,33], then DOWN again to see where the blocks go.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: rows [30,31] cols [39,43]
# Need to reach white cross at rows [46,47] cols [51,52]
#
# Let me try DOWN from [39,43] to [29,33], then explore from there

# First, get full boundary of gray structure
seg = current_frame.segmentation
gray_node = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'G' and n['pixels'] > 1000][0]
print(f"Gray boundary: {gray_node['boundary']}")

# Go DOWN to [29,33]
predict(expect_change=True, note='DOWN to center')
action(['DOWN'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Orange: rows [{min(rows)},{max(rows)}] cols [{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")

# Try DOWN from [29,33]
predict(expect_change=True, note='DOWN from center')
action(['DOWN'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Orange: rows [{min(rows)},{max(rows)}] cols [{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")

# Try RIGHT from new position
predict(expect_change=True, note='RIGHT')
action(['RIGHT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Orange: rows [{min(rows)},{max(rows)}] cols [{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")

# Try DOWN
predict(expect_change=True, note='DOWN')
action(['DOWN'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Orange: rows [{min(rows)},{max(rows)}] cols [{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")

print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
